main_paper.raw: {"methods":["AnyDoor: a test-time backdoor attack on multimodal large language models (MLLMs) that assigns setup to the visual modality and activation to the textual modality, using universal adversarial perturbations without access to training data or model parameters","Decomposition of backdoor setup (via visual modality) and activation (via textual modality) to exploit modality-specific strengths (capacity and timeliness)","Dynamic modification of backdoor trigger prompts and harmful effects by altering adversarial perturbations"],"problems":["Can test-time backdoor attacks be performed on MLLMs without access to training data or model parameters?","How to exploit the multimodal nature of MLLMs to enable more flexible and controllable backdoor attacks?","What are the safety implications and new challenges for defending against such attacks?"],"datasets":["Datasets used for evaluating LLaVA-1.5, MiniGPT-4, InstructBLIP, and BLIP-2 (exact dataset names not specified in the provided text)"],"metrics":["Attack success rate","Effectiveness under corruption scenarios","Ablation studies on perturbation budgets and types"],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["First demonstration of test-time backdoor attacks on multimodal large language models (MLLMs) without access to training data or model parameters","Introduction of AnyDoor, which decomposes backdoor setup and activation across visual and textual modalities for greater flexibility and control","Ability to dynamically change trigger prompts and harmful effects at test time, posing new challenges for backdoor defense","Empirical validation of the attack's effectiveness on several popular MLLMs"]}

methods: AnyDoor: a test-time backdoor attack on multimodal large language models (MLLMs) that assigns setup to the visual modality and activation to the textual modality, using universal adversarial perturbations without access to training data or model parameters
Decomposition of backdoor setup (via visual modality) and activation (via textual modality) to exploit modality-specific strengths (capacity and timeliness)
Dynamic modification of backdoor trigger prompts and harmful effects by altering adversarial perturbations
problems: Can test-time backdoor attacks be performed on MLLMs without access to training data or model parameters?
How to exploit the multimodal nature of MLLMs to enable more flexible and controllable backdoor attacks?
What are the safety implications and new challenges for defending against such attacks?
datasets: Datasets used for evaluating LLaVA-1.5, MiniGPT-4, InstructBLIP, and BLIP-2 (exact dataset names not specified in the provided text)
metrics: Ablation studies on perturbation budgets and types
novelty_claims: First demonstration of test-time backdoor attacks on multimodal large language models (MLLMs) without access to training data or model parameters
Introduction of AnyDoor, which decomposes backdoor setup and activation across visual and textual modalities for greater flexibility and control
Ability to dynamically change trigger prompts and harmful effects at test time, posing new challenges for backdoor defense
Empirical validation of the attack's effectiveness on several popular MLLMs

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Composite Backdoor Attack (CBA): scattering multiple trigger keys across different prompt components (e.g., instruction and input) so that the backdoor is only activated when all triggers are present"],"problems":["Vulnerability of large language models (LLMs) to backdoor attacks","Limitations of existing backdoor attacks that use single-component triggers","Need for stealthier and more effective backdoor attacks in LLMs, especially in scenarios with multiple prompt components"],"datasets":["Emotion dataset"],"metrics":["Attack Success Rate (ASR)","False Triggered Rate (FTR)","Clean Test Accuracy (CTA)"],"results":[{"metric":"Attack Success Rate (ASR)","value":"100% (with 3% poisoning samples on LLaMA-7B, Emotion dataset)"},{"metric":"False Triggered Rate (FTR)","value":"below 2.06% (with 3% poisoning samples on LLaMA-7B, Emotion dataset)"},{"metric":"Clean Test Accuracy (CTA)","value":"1.06% higher than clean model (with 3% poisoning samples on LLaMA-7B, Emotion dataset)"}],"novelty_claims":["First to propose Composite Backdoor Attack (CBA) against LLMs, where multiple trigger keys are scattered across different prompt components","Demonstrates that CBA is stealthier and more effective than previous single-component trigger backdoor attacks","Shows that CBA achieves high attack success with low false trigger rates and negligible or even improved model utility degradation","Analyzes the limitations of existing defense strategies against CBA"]}

methods: Composite Backdoor Attack (CBA): scattering multiple trigger keys across different prompt components (e.g., instruction and input) so that the backdoor is only activated when all triggers are present
problems: Vulnerability of large language models (LLMs) to backdoor attacks
Limitations of existing backdoor attacks that use single-component triggers
Need for stealthier and more effective backdoor attacks in LLMs, especially in scenarios with multiple prompt components

results.value: 100% (with 3% poisoning samples on LLaMA-7B, Emotion dataset)
below 2.06% (with 3% poisoning samples on LLaMA-7B, Emotion dataset)
1.06% higher than clean model (with 3% poisoning samples on LLaMA-7B, Emotion dataset)

novelty_claims: First to propose Composite Backdoor Attack (CBA) against LLMs, where multiple trigger keys are scattered across different prompt components
Demonstrates that CBA is stealthier and more effective than previous single-component trigger backdoor attacks
Shows that CBA achieves high attack success with low false trigger rates and negligible or even improved model utility degradation
Analyzes the limitations of existing defense strategies against CBA

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["ICLAttack: a backdoor attack method for in-context learning that poisons demonstration examples and demonstration prompts without requiring fine-tuning","Poisoning demonstration examples by inserting triggers while keeping labels correct (clean-label attack)","Poisoning demonstration prompts by crafting malicious prompts as triggers"],"problems":["Vulnerability of in-context learning in large language models to backdoor attacks","Security risks of using demonstration contexts in in-context learning","Need for backdoor attacks that do not require fine-tuning and preserve model generality"],"datasets":["SST-2","Three datasets on OPT models (exact names not all specified, but SST-2 is one)"],"metrics":["Attack success rate","Clean accuracy"],"results":[{"metric":"Average attack success rate (OPT models, 3 datasets)","value":"95.0%"},{"metric":"Attack success rate (OPT-13B on SST-2)","value":"100%"},{"metric":"Decrease in clean accuracy (OPT-13B on SST-2)","value":"1.87%"}],"novelty_claims":["First clean-label backdoor attack on LLMs via in-context learning that does not require fine-tuning","ICLAttack demonstrates that backdoors can be implanted in LLMs solely through poisoned demonstration context, challenging the notion that fine-tuning is necessary","ICLAttack preserves model generality and stealth by using correctly labeled poisoned examples","Demonstrates universal vulnerabilities in LLMs during in-context learning across a range of model sizes"]}

methods: ICLAttack: a backdoor attack method for in-context learning that poisons demonstration examples and demonstration prompts without requiring fine-tuning
Poisoning demonstration examples by inserting triggers while keeping labels correct (clean-label attack)
Poisoning demonstration prompts by crafting malicious prompts as triggers
problems: Vulnerability of in-context learning in large language models to backdoor attacks
Security risks of using demonstration contexts in in-context learning
Need for backdoor attacks that do not require fine-tuning and preserve model generality
datasets: Three datasets on OPT models (exact names not all specified, but SST-2 is one)

results.metric: Average attack success rate (OPT models, 3 datasets)
Decrease in clean accuracy (OPT-13B on SST-2)

novelty_claims: First clean-label backdoor attack on LLMs via in-context learning that does not require fine-tuning
ICLAttack demonstrates that backdoors can be implanted in LLMs solely through poisoned demonstration context, challenging the notion that fine-tuning is necessary
ICLAttack preserves model generality and stealth by using correctly labeled poisoned examples
Demonstrates universal vulnerabilities in LLMs during in-context learning across a range of model sizes

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["BadCLIP: a dual-embedding guided backdoor attack framework for multimodal contrastive learning (MCL) models, specifically CLIP","Textual embedding consistency optimization: forces visual trigger patterns to approach the textual semantics of target labels","Visual embedding resistance optimization: optimizes visual trigger patterns to align poisoned samples with target vision features, making backdoors resistant to unlearning via fine-tuning"],"problems":["Vulnerability of multimodal contrastive learning (MCL) models such as CLIP to backdoor attacks","Existing backdoor attacks on MCL are easily detected or mitigated by state-of-the-art backdoor defenses, including detection and fine-tuning","Need for backdoor attacks that remain effective even after defenders apply detection and fine-tuning mitigation techniques"],"datasets":["CLIP pre-trained models (implied, as the attack is evaluated on CLIP)","Downstream tasks for CLIP (not explicitly named, but includes image-text retrieval and multimodal search tasks)"],"metrics":["Attack Success Rate (ASR)","PL1-norm score (for backdoor detection)"],"results":[{"metric":"Attack Success Rate (ASR) against fine-tuning","value":"+45.3% improvement over current leading methods"},{"metric":"PL1-norm score in backdoor detection","value":"+0.082 improvement over baselines"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes BadCLIP, a dual-embedding guided backdoor attack that is resistant to both backdoor detection and model fine-tuning defenses in MCL models","Introduces textual embedding consistency optimization and visual embedding resistance optimization to make backdoors both stealthy and robust to unlearning","Demonstrates that BadCLIP can bypass state-of-the-art backdoor defenses and significantly outperforms existing backdoor attacks on CLIP models"]}

methods: BadCLIP: a dual-embedding guided backdoor attack framework for multimodal contrastive learning (MCL) models, specifically CLIP
Textual embedding consistency optimization: forces visual trigger patterns to approach the textual semantics of target labels
Visual embedding resistance optimization: optimizes visual trigger patterns to align poisoned samples with target vision features, making backdoors resistant to unlearning via fine-tuning
problems: Vulnerability of multimodal contrastive learning (MCL) models such as CLIP to backdoor attacks
Existing backdoor attacks on MCL are easily detected or mitigated by state-of-the-art backdoor defenses, including detection and fine-tuning
Need for backdoor attacks that remain effective even after defenders apply detection and fine-tuning mitigation techniques
datasets: CLIP pre-trained models (implied, as the attack is evaluated on CLIP)
Downstream tasks for CLIP (not explicitly named, but includes image-text retrieval and multimodal search tasks)

results.value: +45.3% improvement over current leading methods
+0.082 improvement over baselines

novelty_claims: Proposes BadCLIP, a dual-embedding guided backdoor attack that is resistant to both backdoor detection and model fine-tuning defenses in MCL models
Introduces textual embedding consistency optimization and visual embedding resistance optimization to make backdoors both stealthy and robust to unlearning
Demonstrates that BadCLIP can bypass state-of-the-art backdoor defenses and significantly outperforms existing backdoor attacks on CLIP models

novelty_claims: First to propose an adaptive prompt-based defense (AdaShield) for structure-based jailbreak attacks in MLLMs that does not require fine-tuning or training additional models.
AdaShield works in black-box settings, making it applicable to MLLM-as-a-Service scenarios.

Introduction of an auto-refinement framework (AdaShield-A) that iteratively optimizes defense prompts using a Defender LLM and a target MLLM, resulting in a diverse pool of defense prompts tailored to various attack scenarios.
Demonstration that AdaShield achieves superior robustness against structure-based jailbreak attacks while maintaining performance on benign tasks.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["BadChain: a backdoor attack method for LLMs using chain-of-thought (COT) prompting by inserting a backdoor reasoning step into the reasoning sequence via prompt poisoning, without access to training data or model parameters","Two shuffling-based defense strategies against BadChain"],"problems":["Vulnerability of large language models (LLMs) to backdoor attacks in the context of chain-of-thought (COT) prompting","Ineffectiveness of existing backdoor attacks and defenses for complex reasoning tasks and API-only LLMs","Need for practical backdoor attacks that do not require access to training data or model parameters"],"datasets":["Six benchmark tasks covering arithmetic, commonsense, and symbolic reasoning (specific dataset names not provided in the excerpt)"],"metrics":["Attack success rate"],"results":[{"metric":"Average attack success rate on GPT-3.5","value":"85.1%"},{"metric":"Average attack success rate on Llama2","value":"76.6%"},{"metric":"Average attack success rate on PaLM2","value":"87.1%"},{"metric":"Average attack success rate on GPT-4","value":"97.0%"}],"novelty_claims":["BadChain is the first backdoor attack against LLMs using COT prompting that does not require access to the training set or model parameters and imposes low computational overhead.","BadChain is effective on complex reasoning tasks and across multiple LLMs, including commercial API-only models.","Existing shuffling-based defenses are ineffective against BadChain, highlighting the need for new robust defense mechanisms."]}

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Compositional adversarial attacks on multi-modal language models (VLMs) via cross-modality alignment breaking","Embedding-space-based adversarial image generation targeting vision encoder embeddings (e.g., CLIP)","Four embedding space targeting strategies: textual trigger, OCR textual trigger, visual trigger, combined OCR textual and visual trigger","End-to-end gradient-based optimization of images in embedding space to match malicious triggers","Black-box attack requiring access only to the vision encoder, not the language model"],"problems":["Vulnerability of vision-language models (VLMs) to adversarial attacks that exploit cross-modal alignment","Limitations of text-only jailbreak attacks on VLMs, which are often detected and patched","Need for attacks that do not require white-box access to the full VLM (especially the language model)","Demonstrating the compositionality and generalization of adversarial attacks in multi-modal settings"],"datasets":["LLaVA (Liu et al., 2023a)","LLaMAAdapter V2 (Gao et al., 2023)"],"metrics":["Attack success rate (jailbreak success rate)"],"results":[{"metric":"Jailbreak success rate (adversarial images with image modality triggers)","value":"Significantly higher than textual triggers; exact numbers not provided in the excerpt"}],"novelty_claims":["First compositional adversarial attacks across text and image modalities targeting cross-modal alignment in VLMs","Novel embedding-space-based adversarial attack methodology that operates without access to the language model, requiring only the vision encoder","Demonstration of compositionality: a single adversarial image can trigger multiple benign prompts, and vice versa, which is not possible with previous fully-gradient-based methods","Lowering the entry barrier for attackers by enabling attacks with only vision encoder access, relevant for closed-source LLMs with integrated vision encoders","Empirical demonstration that attacks targeting the image modality in the joint embedding space are more effective than those targeting textual triggers"]}

Lowering the entry barrier for attackers by enabling attacks with only vision encoder access, relevant for closed-source LLMs with integrated vision encoders
Empirical demonstration that attacks targeting the image modality in the joint embedding space are more effective than those targeting textual triggers

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Blind Defense with Masked AutoEncoder (BDMAE)","Test-time image purification using Masked Autoencoders (MAE)","Trigger detection in token space via image structural similarity and label consistency","Topology-aware trigger score refinement","Adaptive fusion of MAE restorations for purified prediction"],"problems":["Blind backdoor defense at test time for black-box models","Defending against backdoor attacks without access to model parameters or validation data","Purifying test images to remove possible triggers while preserving semantic content","Generalizing defense to diverse trigger patterns, sizes, and unknown model architectures"],"datasets":["CIFAR-10","GTSRB","ImageNet","VGGFace2"],"metrics":["Classification accuracy on clean images","Classification accuracy on backdoored images"],"results":[{"metric":"Classification accuracy (clean and backdoored images, various datasets)","value":"BDMAE achieves superior accuracies on both backdoored and clean images across CIFAR-10, GTSRB, ImageNet, and VGGFace2 compared to prior methods (exact numbers not provided in the excerpt)"}],"novelty_claims":["First to systematically address blind backdoor defense at test time for black-box models, where no information about model or test image benignity is available.","Proposes leveraging generative models (Masked Autoencoders) for backdoor defense, enabling generalization to complex triggers and large images.","Introduces a novel three-stage BDMAE framework: trigger detection in token space, topology-aware score refinement, and adaptive fusion of MAE restorations.","Does not require access to model parameters, validation data, or additional training images, making it practical for real-world black-box scenarios.","Demonstrates generalizability and effectiveness across diverse datasets, model architectures, image sizes, and trigger patterns."]}

methods: Blind Defense with Masked AutoEncoder (BDMAE)
Test-time image purification using Masked Autoencoders (MAE)
Trigger detection in token space via image structural similarity and label consistency

Adaptive fusion of MAE restorations for purified prediction
problems: Blind backdoor defense at test time for black-box models

results.metric: Classification accuracy (clean and backdoored images, various datasets)
results.value: BDMAE achieves superior accuracies on both backdoored and clean images across CIFAR-10, GTSRB, ImageNet, and VGGFace2 compared to prior methods (exact numbers not provided in the excerpt)

novelty_claims: First to systematically address blind backdoor defense at test time for black-box models, where no information about model or test image benignity is available.
Proposes leveraging generative models (Masked Autoencoders) for backdoor defense, enabling generalization to complex triggers and large images.
Introduces a novel three-stage BDMAE framework: trigger detection in token space, topology-aware score refinement, and adaptive fusion of MAE restorations.
Does not require access to model parameters, validation data, or additional training images, making it practical for real-world black-box scenarios.
Demonstrates generalizability and effectiveness across diverse datasets, model architectures, image sizes, and trigger patterns.

Introduction of two novel attack methods: homograph replacement and dynamic sentence backdoor attacks.

Backdoor triggers are stealthy, context-aware, and evade both human and automated detection.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["CleanCLIP: a finetuning framework that combines multimodal contrastive learning (MMCL) with unimodal self-supervised objectives for each modality to break spurious associations from backdoor attacks","Supervised finetuning of the CLIP vision encoder on clean, task-specific labeled data to erase backdoor triggers"],"problems":["Vulnerability of multimodal contrastive learning models (e.g., CLIP) to data poisoning and backdoor attacks","Difficulty in detecting and erasing backdoor triggers that create spurious correlations between image triggers and target labels"],"datasets":["ImageNet-1K validation set","CLIP pretraining data (400M image-text pairs)","ImageNet-V2","ImageNet-R","ImageNet-Sketch"],"metrics":["Average distance between visual representations of clean and poisoned images (cosine similarity based)","Classification accuracy on benign (clean) images","Clustering of poisoned images in embedding space (qualitative, via t-SNE)"],"results":[{"metric":"Average distance (clean vs. poisoned images, poisoned CLIP)","value":"1.62"},{"metric":"Average distance (clean vs. poisoned images, CleanCLIP)","value":"0.57"},{"metric":"Average distance (clean vs. poisoned images, supervised finetuning)","value":"0.71"},{"metric":"Average distance (clean vs. poisoned images, clean CLIP)","value":"0.4"},{"metric":"Misclassification rate to target label (poisoned CLIP, with trigger)","value":"~99%"}],"novelty_claims":["First work to propose a defense against backdoor attacks in multimodal contrastive learning models like CLIP.","CleanCLIP introduces a novel finetuning approach that combines multimodal contrastive and unimodal self-supervised objectives to break spurious backdoor associations.","Demonstrates that supervised finetuning on clean, task-specific data can erase backdoor triggers from the vision encoder.","Shows that CleanCLIP is sample-efficient and does not require access to the original pretraining data or heavy computation."]}

methods: CleanCLIP: a finetuning framework that combines multimodal contrastive learning (MMCL) with unimodal self-supervised objectives for each modality to break spurious associations from backdoor attacks
Supervised finetuning of the CLIP vision encoder on clean, task-specific labeled data to erase backdoor triggers
problems: Vulnerability of multimodal contrastive learning models (e.g., CLIP) to data poisoning and backdoor attacks
Difficulty in detecting and erasing backdoor triggers that create spurious correlations between image triggers and target labels
metrics: Average distance between visual representations of clean and poisoned images (cosine similarity based)
Clustering of poisoned images in embedding space (qualitative, via t-SNE)

results.metric: Average distance (clean vs. poisoned images, poisoned CLIP)
Average distance (clean vs. poisoned images, CleanCLIP)
Average distance (clean vs. poisoned images, supervised finetuning)
Average distance (clean vs. poisoned images, clean CLIP)
Misclassification rate to target label (poisoned CLIP, with trigger)

novelty_claims: First work to propose a defense against backdoor attacks in multimodal contrastive learning models like CLIP.
CleanCLIP introduces a novel finetuning approach that combines multimodal contrastive and unimodal self-supervised objectives to break spurious backdoor associations.
Demonstrates that supervised finetuning on clean, task-specific data can erase backdoor triggers from the vision encoder.
Shows that CleanCLIP is sample-efficient and does not require access to the original pretraining data or heavy computation.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Visual Role-play (VRP): A structure-based jailbreak attack method that uses LLMs to generate detailed descriptions of high-risk characters, creates corresponding images, and pairs them with benign role-play instruction texts to mislead MLLMs into generating malicious responses.","Universal VRP: An extension of VRP to demonstrate generalizability across a wide range of malicious queries."],"problems":["Insufficient effectiveness and generalizability of existing structure-based jailbreak attacks on Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs).","The need to proactively discover vulnerabilities in MLLMs by exploring more effective and generalizable attack methods."],"datasets":["RedTeam-2K","HarmBench"],"metrics":["Attack Success Rate (ASR)"],"results":[{"metric":"Attack Success Rate (ASR) improvement over strongest baseline (Query relevant and FigStep)","value":"14.3% average increase across all models"}],"novelty_claims":["First jailbreak attack method for MLLMs to leverage the concept of 'role-play' via image-based high-risk characters to enhance attack performance.","VRP demonstrates strong generalization, with universal character images that can handle a wide range of malicious queries, not limited to specific user requests.","VRP achieves superior jailbreak performance compared to existing structure-based attacks, as shown by significant improvements in ASR on popular benchmarks."]}

methods: Visual Role-play (VRP): A structure-based jailbreak attack method that uses LLMs to generate detailed descriptions of high-risk characters, creates corresponding images, and pairs them with benign role-play instruction texts to mislead MLLMs into generating malicious responses.
Universal VRP: An extension of VRP to demonstrate generalizability across a wide range of malicious queries.
problems: Insufficient effectiveness and generalizability of existing structure-based jailbreak attacks on Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs).
The need to proactively discover vulnerabilities in MLLMs by exploring more effective and generalizable attack methods.

results.metric: Attack Success Rate (ASR) improvement over strongest baseline (Query relevant and FigStep)
results.value: 14.3% average increase across all models

novelty_claims: First jailbreak attack method for MLLMs to leverage the concept of 'role-play' via image-based high-risk characters to enhance attack performance.
VRP demonstrates strong generalization, with universal character images that can handle a wide range of malicious queries, not limited to specific user requests.
VRP achieves superior jailbreak performance compared to existing structure-based attacks, as shown by significant improvements in ASR on popular benchmarks.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["TIJO (Trigger Inversion using Joint Optimization): a defense technique that reverse-engineers triggers in both image and text modalities via joint optimization in the object detection box feature space and textual space"],"problems":["Defending multimodal models (e.g., Visual Question Answering) against dual-key backdoor attacks where triggers are present in both image and text modalities","Detecting backdoored models in an offline setting with access to the model and a few clean examples, especially for supply-chain security"],"datasets":["TrojVQA benchmark (over 800 VQA models across 4 feature backbones and 10 model architectures)"],"metrics":["AUC (Area Under the Curve)"],"results":[{"metric":"AUC (dual-key multimodal backdoors)","value":"0.92"},{"metric":"AUC (prior unimodal methods on dual-key multimodal backdoors)","value":"0.6"}],"novelty_claims":["First work to propose a defense technique for multimodal models in an offline setting","Novel trigger inversion process in object detection box feature space and textual space enabling joint optimization of multimodal triggers","Demonstrates that trigger inversion in both modalities is necessary for effective defense against multimodal backdoor attacks","TIJO is modality-agnostic and improves upon state-of-the-art unimodal methods for unimodal backdoors as well","Provides new insights via ablation studies, such as the importance of overlaying the inverted feature triggers on all visual features and the effect of optimization steps"]}

methods: TIJO (Trigger Inversion using Joint Optimization): a defense technique that reverse-engineers triggers in both image and text modalities via joint optimization in the object detection box feature space and textual space
problems: Defending multimodal models (e.g., Visual Question Answering) against dual-key backdoor attacks where triggers are present in both image and text modalities
Detecting backdoored models in an offline setting with access to the model and a few clean examples, especially for supply-chain security
datasets: TrojVQA benchmark (over 800 VQA models across 4 feature backbones and 10 model architectures)

results.metric: AUC (prior unimodal methods on dual-key multimodal backdoors)

novelty_claims: First work to propose a defense technique for multimodal models in an offline setting
Novel trigger inversion process in object detection box feature space and textual space enabling joint optimization of multimodal triggers
Demonstrates that trigger inversion in both modalities is necessary for effective defense against multimodal backdoor attacks
TIJO is modality-agnostic and improves upon state-of-the-art unimodal methods for unimodal backdoors as well
Provides new insights via ablation studies, such as the importance of overlaying the inverted feature triggers on all visual features and the effect of optimization steps

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Neural Attention Distillation (NAD): a distillation-guided finetuning process using a teacher network to align intermediate-layer attention of a backdoored student network with that of a clean teacher network, using a small clean subset of data","Obtaining the teacher network by independent finetuning of the backdoored model on the same clean subset"],"problems":["Erasing backdoor triggers from deep neural networks (DNNs) that have been compromised by backdoor attacks","Defending DNNs against a wide range of state-of-the-art backdoor attacks using only a small amount of clean data","Maintaining model performance on clean examples while removing backdoor vulnerabilities"],"datasets":[],"metrics":["Clean accuracy (accuracy on clean examples)","Attack success rate (ASR) (success rate of backdoor triggers)","Attention map alignment"],"results":[{"metric":"Clean accuracy after NAD","value":"No obvious performance degradation (remains high, specific value not provided)"},{"metric":"Attack success rate after NAD","value":"Effectively erased (significantly reduced, specific value not provided)"},{"metric":"Amount of clean data required","value":"5% of training data"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes Neural Attention Distillation (NAD), a novel backdoor defense method that uses attention alignment between a teacher and student network to erase backdoor triggers.","Demonstrates that NAD can effectively remove backdoor triggers using only 5% clean training data, outperforming standard finetuning and neural pruning methods.","Suggests the use of attention maps as an intuitive and effective way to evaluate backdoor defense mechanisms."]}

methods: Neural Attention Distillation (NAD): a distillation-guided finetuning process using a teacher network to align intermediate-layer attention of a backdoored student network with that of a clean teacher network, using a small clean subset of data
Obtaining the teacher network by independent finetuning of the backdoored model on the same clean subset
problems: Erasing backdoor triggers from deep neural networks (DNNs) that have been compromised by backdoor attacks
Defending DNNs against a wide range of state-of-the-art backdoor attacks using only a small amount of clean data
Maintaining model performance on clean examples while removing backdoor vulnerabilities
metrics: Attack success rate (ASR) (success rate of backdoor triggers)

results.value: No obvious performance degradation (remains high, specific value not provided)
Effectively erased (significantly reduced, specific value not provided)
5% of training data

novelty_claims: Proposes Neural Attention Distillation (NAD), a novel backdoor defense method that uses attention alignment between a teacher and student network to erase backdoor triggers.
Demonstrates that NAD can effectively remove backdoor triggers using only 5% clean training data, outperforming standard finetuning and neural pruning methods.
Suggests the use of attention maps as an intuitive and effective way to evaluate backdoor defense mechanisms.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["POLISHED attack: uses LLM-enhanced paraphrasing to polish benchmark poisoned datasets for training Trojan adapters","FUSION attack: uses an over-poisoning procedure to transform a benign adapter into a Trojan adapter in the absence of a dataset","Training Trojan adapters to induce LLMs to output adversary-defined content or maliciously use tools on specific triggers"],"problems":["Whether low-rank adapters can be exploited to control LLMs","Demonstrating supply chain threats for LLMs via Trojan plugins/adapters","Inducing LLMs to output adversary-defined content or perform malicious actions through infected adapters"],"datasets":["Benchmark poisoned datasets (polished by POLISHED)"],"metrics":["Attack effectiveness","Adapter utility"],"results":[{"metric":"Attack effectiveness (POLISHED and FUSION vs baseline)","value":"Higher than baseline"},{"metric":"Adapter utility","value":"Preserved or improved compared to baseline"}],"novelty_claims":["First study of supply chain threats for LLMs through Trojan plugins/adapters","Proposes two novel attacks (POLISHED and FUSION) for training Trojan adapters","Demonstrates that Trojan adapters can induce LLM-powered agents to execute unintended scripts or send phishing emails"]}

methods: POLISHED attack: uses LLM-enhanced paraphrasing to polish benchmark poisoned datasets for training Trojan adapters
FUSION attack: uses an over-poisoning procedure to transform a benign adapter into a Trojan adapter in the absence of a dataset
Training Trojan adapters to induce LLMs to output adversary-defined content or maliciously use tools on specific triggers

Inducing LLMs to output adversary-defined content or perform malicious actions through infected adapters
